critique.summary: The article presents an interactive 3D simulator of the three-body problem that runs in a web browser. It appears to allow users to manipulate parameters and observe the complex gravitational interactions between three bodies in real time.
mainPoints: An interactive 3D simulation of the three-body problem is available in-browser.
Users can adjust physical parameters such as gravitational constant (G), softness, and time step (dt).
The simulator uses numerical integration methods to compute the trajectories of the bodies.
critique.truthfulness: The three-body problem is a well-known problem in classical mechanics involving the prediction of the motion of three gravitationally interacting bodies. Interactive simulations of this problem are common and feasible with modern web technologies. The parameters mentioned, such as gravitational constant and time step, are standard in numerical simulations of gravitational systems. The claims about the simulator's capabilities are consistent with established scientific understanding and common computational approaches.
considerations: The article does not specify which numerical integration method is used, which affects accuracy and stability.
No information is provided about the source code, validation, or comparison with analytical or other numerical results.
The simulator's performance and limitations on different browsers or devices are not discussed.
There is no mention of educational or research applications or how this tool compares to existing simulators.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around a browser-based 3D three-body problem simulator, with users commenting on its features, solver accuracy, and UI issues. Some share related projects and technical insights about numerical methods used in simulations.

comments.author: hazrmard
comments.text: Very cool! Interesting how the choice of solver affects the solution. Euler doesn't handle misbehaved equations very well. You can see this in the Helix setup where the bodies just fly off.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies the limitations of the Euler method in numerical simulations and provides a relevant example, showing good understanding of the topic.

comments.author: westurner
comments.text: Notes re: Multi-body problems on "Show HN: Browser-based interactive 3D Three-Body problem simulator" (2025) https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45981336 which has Velocity Verlet integration method, 4th-order Runge-Kutta (RK4), and Kepler solvers
comments.analysis: Provides useful information about advanced numerical methods relevant to the three-body problem, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: ubezon
comments.text: Very cool! nice to be able to export json too
comments.analysis: Expresses positive feedback and highlights a useful feature of the simulator, though it lacks technical depth.

comments.author: neondude
comments.text: Feedback : The ui covers more than half my screen on mobile browser
comments.analysis: Provides practical user feedback about UI issues on mobile devices, which is valuable for improving usability.

comments.author: hermitcrab
comments.text: Random3D seems to always have all the stars heading off to infinity.
comments.analysis: Observes a behavior in the simulation that may indicate instability or solver issues, contributing useful insight.

comments.text: The tri-Solarians could have just used this!
comments.analysis: A humorous, off-topic comment referencing science fiction, adding little substantive value.

comments.analysis: A brief, unclear comment that lacks context or constructive content.
